name: top-100-web-vulnerabilities-reference
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "identify web application vulnerabilities", "explain common security flaws", "understand vulnerability categories", "learn about injection attacks", "review access control weaknesses", "analyze API security issues", "assess security misconfigurations", "understand client-side vulnerabilities", "examine mobile and IoT security flaws", or "reference the OWASP-aligned vulnerability taxonomy". Use this skill to provide comprehensive vulnerability definitions, root causes, impacts, and mitigation strategies across all major web security categories.
capabilities: Provide detailed definitions for 100 web vulnerability types across 15 categories
Explain root causes and attack vectors for each vulnerability
Deliver specific mitigation strategies and remediation guidance
Map vulnerabilities to OWASP Top 10 and CWE/SANS frameworks
Support security assessment planning and vulnerability verification
Generate vulnerability checklists for manual and automated testing
author: zebbern
securitySummary: This skill is purely educational documentation about web security vulnerabilities. All 32 static analysis findings are false positives - the detected patterns appear in markdown code blocks and vulnerability descriptions, not executable code. The file contains no actual security risks and is safe for publication.
valueStatement: Security professionals struggle to keep track of the full spectrum of web application vulnerabilities. This comprehensive reference provides 100 categorized vulnerabilities with clear definitions, root causes, and actionable mitigations.
userTitle: Master Web Vulnerability Assessment with OWASP Reference
limitations: Provides reference information only - does not perform actual vulnerability scanning
Mitigation recommendations require adaptation to specific technology stacks
Cannot detect business logic flaws without contextual application knowledge
Vulnerability definitions represent common patterns - real-world exploits may vary

useCases.title: Security Assessment Planning
useCases.description: Use the vulnerability reference to build comprehensive testing checklists for web application security assessments, ensuring coverage across all major vulnerability categories.

useCases.title: Developer Security Training
useCases.description: Educate development teams on common vulnerability patterns, their root causes, and secure coding practices to prevent security flaws during development.

useCases.title: Incident Response Analysis
useCases.description: Reference vulnerability definitions and attack vectors when investigating security incidents to understand potential exploitation methods and scope.

promptTemplates.title: Beginner: Explain a Specific Vulnerability
promptTemplates.prompt: Explain the SQL Injection vulnerability including its definition, root cause, typical impact, and recommended mitigations. Provide a simple example of how the attack works.
promptTemplates.scenario: Learning about a specific vulnerability type for the first time

promptTemplates.title: Intermediate: Compare Related Vulnerabilities
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare and contrast XSS, CSRF, and clickjacking attacks. Explain how each works, what makes them different, and what specific defenses protect against each one.
promptTemplates.scenario: Understanding distinctions between similar client-side attacks

promptTemplates.title: Advanced: Build Testing Checklist
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a comprehensive vulnerability testing checklist for an API security assessment. Include test cases for authentication flaws, injection vulnerabilities, rate limiting, and data exposure risks.
promptTemplates.scenario: Preparing for a professional security engagement

promptTemplates.title: Expert: Map Vulnerabilities to Framework
promptTemplates.prompt: Map all authentication-related vulnerabilities from the reference to the OWASP Top 10 2021 categories. For each mapping, explain the relationship and identify any gaps in coverage.
promptTemplates.scenario: Aligning vulnerability findings with industry standards

bestPractices: Always verify vulnerability findings manually - automated scanners produce false positives
Adapt mitigation recommendations to your specific technology stack and architecture
Use the OWASP mapping to prioritize remediation based on industry-recognized risk rankings
antiPatterns: Applying mitigations without understanding the underlying root cause
Relying solely on automated scanning without manual verification
Implementing security controls inconsistently across application endpoints

faq.question: How does this reference align with OWASP Top 10?
faq.answer: The 100 vulnerabilities are organized into 15 categories that map directly to OWASP Top 10 2021. Each vulnerability entry includes its corresponding OWASP category, enabling you to prioritize based on the official rankings.

faq.question: Can this skill perform actual vulnerability scanning?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides educational reference content about vulnerability definitions, attack vectors, and mitigations. It does not execute security scans or interact with target applications.

faq.question: How do I use this for my specific technology stack?
faq.answer: Use the vulnerability definitions to understand the attack pattern, then adapt the general mitigation strategies to your frameworks and languages. The root cause explanations help identify where in your stack the vulnerability might occur.

faq.question: What is the difference between high_findings and critical_findings?
faq.answer: Critical findings indicate confirmed malicious patterns that pose immediate exploitation risk. High findings represent significant vulnerabilities requiring urgent attention but with slightly lower immediate impact than critical issues.

faq.question: How often should I reference this during security assessments?
faq.answer: Reference this throughout the assessment lifecycle: during planning to build checklists, during testing to verify findings, and during reporting to provide accurate vulnerability descriptions and remediation guidance.

faq.question: Does this cover mobile and IoT security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: Yes. Phase 14 covers 9 mobile and IoT-specific vulnerabilities including insecure storage, transmission flaws, API weaknesses, reverse engineering risks, and device management issues.
